title: Data Types
summary: Choose the kind of value a variable is allowed to hold.
refresher: Java has small built-in types such as `int`, `double`, `boolean`, and `char`. `int` is for whole numbers. `double` is for decimals. `boolean` is true or false. `String` is text, but it is an object, not a primitive type.

blocks.body: A type tells Java what kind of value a variable can hold. That lets Java catch many mistakes before the program runs. If a variable is an `int`, Java will not quietly place a sentence inside it.

blocks.caption: Use int for whole numbers, double for decimals, boolean for true/false values, char for one character, and String for text.

blocks.title: The five you will use constantly
items: `int` holds a whole number: 42, 0, -7. No decimal point, ever.
`double` holds a number with a decimal point: 3.75, 2.0, -0.5.
`boolean` holds exactly one of two values: `true` or `false`, lowercase, no quotes.
`char` holds one single character in single quotes: 'A', '7', '?'.
`String` holds text in double quotes: "Ada", "7", "hello world".
blocks.body: The type also affects math. When Java divides two `int` values, the result is still an `int`. The decimal part is dropped. If you need a decimal answer, make at least one number a `double`.

blocks.caption: The first division uses two ints, so Java keeps only the whole-number part. The second uses a double, so the decimal survives.

blocks.title: Casting changes how Java treats a value
blocks.body: Casting means asking Java to treat a value as another type. Use it when you mean it. Casting can also throw information away, like turning `3.9` into `3`.

Now the part of this lesson that will cost you points on an assignment if you skip it. When Java divides two `int` values, the answer is an `int`. There is no room for a decimal inside an `int`, so Java throws the decimal part away and keeps the whole number.

blocks.caption: Same numbers, three different answers. The type of the result is decided by the types you divided, not by the value you were hoping for. One `double` anywhere in the division is enough to get a decimal back.

blocks.title: 5 / 2 is 2, and Java never mentions it
blocks.body: This is called integer division. It does not round to the nearest whole number, it chops the decimal off, so 5 / 2 is 2 and 9 / 10 is 0. Nothing is underlined, nothing crashes, and your program prints its answer with total confidence. When a number comes out lower than you expected, or comes out as a flat 0, check whether both sides of the `/` were ints.

blocks.caption: The left version looks like it should work. `average` is declared as a `double`, so surely the answer is a decimal. It is not. Java finishes the division first, while both sides are still ints, and 7 / 2 is 3. Only then is that 3 stored into a `double`, which turns it into 3.0. The decimal was already gone. That trailing .0 is what makes the bug so slippery: it came from the type you declared, not from the math.

blocks.prompt: A student is working out what percent of the points they earned. What does this print?
blocks.why: Look at what is inside the parentheses. `earned / possible` is 3 / 4, and both are ints, so the answer has to be an int. Java chops the decimal and gets 0. Then 0 * 100 is 0, and storing that in a `double` prints 0.0. If you picked 75.0, you did the math the way a person would, and that is exactly the trap: the parentheses forced the division to happen while both sides were still ints. Write `((double) earned / possible) * 100` and you get 75.0.

blocks.caption: Turning one type into another is called casting. You write the type you want in parentheses in front of the value. Casting a `double` to an `int` truncates, the same way integer division does, so 9.99 becomes 9 and not 10. Do not reach for a cast when you meant to round. Going the other way, `int` into `double`, needs no cast at all, because nothing is lost.

blocks.title: Java will not quietly shrink a value for you
blocks.body: Write `int n = 9.99;` and it does not compile. The message is `possible lossy conversion from double to int`. Java is telling you a `double` does not fit inside an `int`, and it will not guess whether you wanted to lose the .99. Adding the cast, `int n = (int) 9.99;`, is you saying yes, throw it away. Now it compiles, and n is 9.

blocks.caption: Java is statically typed. Before your program runs, the compiler reads every line and checks that the types line up. Here the print sits above the bad line, and you still never see the word starting. Not one line ran, because the compiler rejected the file before it produced anything to run. In Python a mix-up like this blows up mid-run, after some output has already appeared. In Java it is caught first, so if you see none of your output at all, suspect the compiler and not your logic.

blocks.prompt: Which line stops this program from compiling?
choices: `double b = 5;`, because 5 has no decimal point
`String c = "5";`, because "5" is a number in quotes
`int d = c;`, because a String cannot be stored in an int
None of them, all four lines are fine
blocks.why: `int d = c;` is the line Java rejects, with `incompatible types: String cannot be converted to int`. It is a compile error and not a crash, so the program never starts and none of the other lines run either. The other three are all legal. `double b = 5;` works because an `int` widens into a `double` with nothing lost, and it stores 5.0. `String c = "5";` is fine too: the quotes make it text, and text is what a `String` holds. To pull the number out of that text, use `Integer.parseInt(c)`.

blocks.title: Habits that prevent most type bugs in Java
items: Watch integer division before assuming the math is wrong.
